move this whole model package including this file to the policy-clamp common module.

Create a new package called "parameters" as below instead of "models"

So this file moves to

@Jim Hahn
Or this could even move into policy-common somewhere. What's your opinion Jim

Delete this class and raise a separate on policy-models to add the required change to 

./models/models-tosca/src/main/java/org/onap/policy/models/tosca/authorative/concepts/ToscaConceptIdentifier.java


It's always better to reuse the existing code.

This defines the annotation that makes the link to the Policy Framework validation. We should make this annotation generally available to all Policy Framework users.

This should be renamed and moved to policy-common. Please ask Jim Hahn where it should go.



Renaming suggestion: ParameterGroupConstraint

Please avoid calling this "Policy...." as the "Policy" word is too overloaded for us.

This is the validator for the link to the Policy Framework validation. We should make this annotation generally available to all Policy Framework users.

This should be renamed and moved to policy-common. Please ask Jim Hahn where it should go.

Renaming suggestion: ParameterGroupValidator

Please avoid calling this "Policy...." as the "Policy" word is too overloaded for us.
Yes, this should go into policy-common, like RestServerParameters
please see my comments for PolicyValidator

it would go in common-parameters.  The one concern I have is that I don't want to include spring or hibernate in policy-common - maybe an appropriate "scope" will make it so it compiles, without actually making them a dependency of the jar/pom.

OTOH, maybe it should just stay here for now.  We can move it if/when we decide to migrate the other components to spring.

Agree that it should be renamed.

where possible, this should exactly match the field name, thus "host" instead of "Host"

same comment below
to move it in policy-common, it needs only jakarta.validation-api into pom
isValid true when value is null ? is that wanted ? 😊
This is typical, as the null check is normally enforced via the @NotNull annotation in the containing class

So this code can be removed ? this should crash during the constraint validation if it's null no ?, did i get you properly ? Or may be that code can be called in others conditions or to avoid sonar bugs ?
Ok got you, sorry I should have foreseen that, at first glance it looks strange 😄
